no_gap.overall_finding: FAILS the no-gap test — and fails it on the single most important data point in the sample. On 2026-06-22 Alan Greenspan died at 100 (confirmed via WebSearch: Federal Reserve official statement + CBS/NBC/ABC wall-to-wall coverage). That is the day's premier already-arrived mainstream event, and the engine scored it det 91.8 / conf 75.6 / gap +16.2 — the LARGEST positive gap in the entire 100-topic sample, landing it in the Anomalies band (det-conf >= 16). The instrument flagged a confirmed, globally-saturated obituary as an 'early, not-yet-broadly-confirmed' speculative signal. That is precisely the broken behavior the no-gap principle says invalidates the gap metric. The failure is systemic, not a one-off: every mainstream NATION and MEGA-CAP collapses to a near-identical ~45/67 signature badged EMERGING (china 45.6/67.5, india 45.6/67.5, russia 45.5/67.3, france 45.3/67.5, apple 45.2/67.2, openai 45.4/67.1, korea 45.4/67.1) with a uniform ~-22 LAGGING gap — i.e. Confidence systematically exceeds Detection by ~22pts on topics that have unambiguously already arrived. obama collapses worse (24.3/56.7, WATCHING, gap -32.4). Surface-form fragmentation splits single mainstream stories into contradictory scores (hormuz STRONG 72.3/83.2 vs 'strait of hormuz' WATCHING 23.9/54.3). And the gap FIELD is internally incoherent on the mcp-family rows (det-conf = +1.5 but stored gap = -23, a ~24pt contradiction), directly breaking the 'both scores explain each other' instrument test. The pass cases are real and important — trump 94.6/94.2/+0.3, FIFA 93.7/93.7/0.0, iran 86.1/90.1/-4, election 94.7/94.3/+0.4 — proving the dual-pathway anti-collapse gate WORKS when it engages. But it engages only for a privileged top tier; the moment a mainstream topic falls below that tier it collapses to the ~45/67 EMERGING signature. Net verdict: the dual-pathway gate is necessary and demonstrably functioning at the very top, but its anti-collapse protection does not extend across the full mainstream band, and the Greenspan +16.2 anomaly shows it can mis-read even a high-Detection confirmed story as speculative. The gap metric is therefore NOT yet a reliable instrument at the already-arrived end of the curve — it passes for the famous handful and fails for the merely-mainstream majority. Severity: SEVERE (fails on the day's #1 confirmed event plus a systematic ~7-topic collapse pattern), though partially mitigated by genuine passes at the very top.
no_gap.detail:
METHOD: I treated 'unambiguously mainstream' as globally-famous people, mega-cap companies, superpower nations, and confirmed major current events, and confirmed the two non-obvious cases via WebSearch. KEY EXTERNAL CONFIRMATIONS (June 2026): (1) Alan Greenspan died 2026-06-22 at age 100 — Federal Reserve official press release + CBS/NBC/ABC/OPB; this is THE breaking mainstream story on the data's date, which makes its +16.2 anomaly gap the decisive falsification. (2) Deniz Undav and Brian Brobbey are real, currently-trending 2026 World Cup figures (Undav tied Roger Milla's bench-scoring record; Brobbey scored the fastest WC brace since 2006) per ESPN/Yahoo/The National — so their floor-level MONITORING scores (13.5/25.5 and 8.8/16.0) understate genuinely-arrived sports news, though this is the most forgivable miss since live-sports source coverage may be thin.

THE TWO FAILURE SIGNATURES:
- POSITIVE-GAP anomaly on confirmed news: Alan Greenspan det 91.8 / conf 75.6 / gap +16.2 (sole large positive gap; in the Anomalies band). Per the no-gap principle this exact case — 'obviously-mainstream, fully-confirmed topic read as uncertain/early' — is the defined broken state.
- NEGATIVE-GAP mainstream collapse: china/india/russia/france/apple/openai/korea all cluster at det ~45 / conf ~67 / gap ~-22, badged EMERGING despite 7-11 platforms and 70-219 mentions. obama is the extreme (24.3/56.7/-32.4, WATCHING). Confidence systematically exceeds Detection on already-arrived topics — the inverse of the intended 'early = Detection leads' design, and a sign Detection is being clamped below what the footprint justifies.

INTERNAL INCOHERENCE: the mcp family (mcp, claude code, claude-code, model-context-protocol, mcp server) all show det-conf ~ 0 to +7 yet store gap = -17 to -23.5. The gap field does not equal Detection - Confidence on these rows (e.g. mcp: 74.5-73.0 = +1.5 vs stored -23). This is a data-integrity bug in heisenberg_gap independent of the mainstream question.

INVERSE CHECK (niche topics with implausibly HIGH confidence / false-aligned): I found NO egregious false-aligned niche rows — the obscure dev-tool slugs (fastapi 46.2/48.6, langchain 45.5/47.9, ollama 43.7/47.8, vector-database 36.8/46.6, codex 45.6/48.3) all sit LOW on BOTH axes with small gaps, which is the CORRECT 'low conviction' read, not false confidence. SpaceX IPO 2026 (24.9/25.4) and the misc footballers collapse low-on-both — also correct (no false confidence manufactured). So the engine's failure mode is one-directional: it does NOT fabricate confidence for niche topics (good), but it DOES strip detection/confidence from already-arrived mainstream topics outside the top tier (bad). That asymmetry is consistent with the dual-pathway gate working only for the highest-footprint rows and the calibration clamp pulling everything else down.

RELEVANT CODE (per provided context, for follow-up): dual_pathway.blend + the w=mainstream_ratio gate (score_all_topics ~4296-4351) is what protects trump/FIFA; the serve-time recompute gate (apply_calibration ~1122-1155) is what's meant to preserve mainstream rows from collapsing to ~25-28 — the ~45/67 cluster suggests that preservation is NOT firing for these mid-mainstream rows, OR their baseline-relative w fell low enough that the expert pathway dominated and clamped them. The Greenspan +16.2 case specifically needs a same-surge/breaking-news check because Detection (91.8) is correctly high but Confidence (75.6) lags — Persistence hasn't accrued for a 1-day-old event, which is the documented gap-closes-as-P-rises mechanism, but for a wall-to-wall confirmed obituary the instrument should not be reading it as a speculative lead.

Sources: Federal Reserve (federalreserve.gov press release), CBS News, NBC News, ABC/Good Morning America (Greenspan); ESPN, Yahoo Sports, The National (Undav/Brobbey World Cup).

confirmed_errors.reasoning:
CONFIRMED as a real error after adversarial verification.

1) MAINSTREAM CHECK (passed): WebSearch confirms the Strait of Hormuz is unambiguously the dominant mainstream geopolitical/oil story right now — the central chokepoint of the active 2026 Iran war. Sources: Wikipedia "2026 Strait of Hormuz crisis," CNN Business (2026-06-09), Bloomberg, Brookings, Congress.gov CRS, plus IEA characterizing it as the "largest supply disruption in the history of the global oil market." It is the SAME story as the shorter "hormuz" topic, which the sample correctly shows STRONG (det 72.3, conf 83.2, 128 mentions). No basis for a niche/expert or genuine-early-emergence read.

2) MECHANISM CHECK — is the low score explainable by a LEGITIMATE mechanism? No. I tried to refute via dual-pathway (expert/niche origin), data-aging (Enterprise-live vs aged), and thin/new-topic explanations — none apply to a fully-arrived mainstream crisis. The score IS produced by a mechanism, but a defective one. I verified in transfer/gradient_engine_backend.py that normalize_topic() (lines 272-296) only lowercases and strips punctuation; it does NOT canonicalize surface-form variants. There is no alias/consolidation layer in the scoring pipeline: the only alias map (transfer/ai_topic_intelligence.py, lines ~118-237) is a hardcoded AI-DOMAIN taxonomy (agentic coding, MCP, codex) — it does not cover geopolitical topics. Therefore "hormuz", "strait", "strait hormuz", and "strait of hormuz" are stored and scored as INDEPENDENT topics. The full canonical phrase accumulates fewer raw signal hits, so its Detection collapses (23.9) and it falls to the WATCHING floor (gap -30.4) while the shorter token reads STRONG. This is exactly the auditor's diagnosis: fragmentation across surface forms splits ONE mainstream story into divergent scores.

3) MISREPRESENTATION: Attention on the Strait of Hormuz is breakout-level; the det=23.9 / WATCHING reading on the canonical full phrase materially understates it and directly contradicts the engine's own STRONG read on "hormuz." Both conditions are met: clearly mainstream AND score clearly misrepresents attention. The auditor's expected match (~det 72.3, STRONG) is correct.

SEVERITY = medium: the story is NOT missed (the shorter "hormuz" variant correctly carries it as STRONG, so the before-it-arrives thesis and data floor are intact), but the engine emits a stark, self-contradictory floor score on the canonical full phrase of the single biggest current world story — a credibility risk on the institutional surfaces and a systemic surface-form fragmentation issue affecting any multi-word mainstream topic. Not high (not a true miss), not low (dominant story, systemic). Relevant file: C:\Users\acinv\OneDrive\Desktop\CODING PROJECTS\NowTrendin v2.0\transfer\gradient_engine_backend.py (normalize_topic / extract_topics).

MAINSTREAM CONFIRMED via WebSearch: "Belgium vs Iran" is a real 2026 FIFA World Cup Group G fixture played June 21, 2026 (the day before today, 2026-06-22), 0-0 draw at LA Stadium, with extensive top-tier mainstream coverage (ESPN, Sky Sports, FOX Sports, FIFA.com, Sports Illustrated, Wikipedia). It is unambiguously a fully-arrived mainstream live event, not niche/expert/early-emerging.

SCORE IS UNDER-WEIGHT BUT EXPLAINABLE BY A KNOWN, DOCUMENTED MECHANISM — not a hidden bug. In transfer/dual_pathway.py the mainstream pathway requires (a) magnitude from peak mainstream-tier engagement (news rows fall below the magnitude floor, _LOG_FLOOR) and (b) breadth = distinct mainstream communities/outlets, with NEWS_MAINSTREAM_MIN=3 distinct reputable outlets needed for mainstream-confirmed and _BREADTH_FULL=4. detection = (1-w)*expert_detection + w*md. For a sports topic expert_detection ~0 (niche ratio ~0); if the engine has only thin mainstream-tier ingestion at the snapshot, abs_breadth is low, md is low, and w (mainstreaming weight) is low, so BOTH detection (10.2) and confidence (17.7) collapse to the floor. overall<35 then mechanically yields MONITORING (calibration_engine.py:1367). gap = detection-confidence = -7.5 is just 10.2-17.7. So MONITORING and the low-low pair are the math working as written.

CRITICALLY, CLAUDE.md names THIS EXACT topic — "the 'Belgium vs Iran, 2026 World Cup' corroboration case" — as the motivating example for the M/D provenance reweighting (reputable -> half weight, full only on >=2 distinct reputable; research outlets -> Dark Matter), which is explicitly "IN DESIGN (next phase, NOT yet shipped; gated by backtest-before-ship)." The low-low score is the CURRENT EXPECTED behavior that the deferred fix targets, i.e. a known acknowledged limitation, not a regression.

REFUTATION OF THE AUDITOR'S "EXPECTED": the auditor claims the correct output should be "a wide POSITIVE gap (early signal)." That is internally inconsistent. A wide positive gap (det>>conf) is the signature of an EARLY EXPERT/NICHE signal. A fully-arrived MAINSTREAM live event should read HIGH on BOTH axes (magnitude + breadth = "it has arrived"), per dual_pathway.py archetype docs — never a wide gap. The auditor conflates mainstream with early-emerging, which are opposite archetypes in this engine, so its proposed-correct shape is wrong.

NET: topic is mainstream and the score does under-represent its real attention, so is_real_error=true, but it is the precise already-tracked, fix-pending case at the floor (not a wild inversion or systemic misrank), so severity=low. Evidence: NowTrendin v2.0/transfer/dual_pathway.py (attention_magnitude, mainstream_breadth, mainstream_detection, blend lines 106-314), NowTrendin v2.0/transfer/calibration_engine.py:1356-1367 (component->stage), NowTrendin v2.0/CLAUDE.md section 15 lines 311-330 (M/D reweighting IN DESIGN, Belgium vs Iran named).

LIVE PULL: GET /risk/scores?limit=300 returned 88 instruments (response 'total' field), all scored within the last 0.7h (scored_at ~2026-06-23T05:01:31Z). 523KB payload. Universe is FRESH (0 rows >24h old), so staleness is NOT a problem here.

WHAT MAKES SENSE: baseline depth is real (62/88 have >=11 cycles, sufficient_baseline=true universally), the universe is current, and the categories are correctly baseline-relative (AT_BASELINE on all, deviation measured via abnormality/percent_delta). There genuinely are no large absolute movers right now (max percent_delta=5.3%), so 'mostly routine' is a defensible read of a quiet tape — this is NOT simply cold-start masking a big mover.

WHAT DOES NOT: (1) The user-facing 'tier' (market_gradient.tier / risk_stage) is a near-constant ROUTINE (98.9%) and disagrees with the 'classification' field that actually moves (WATCH/ELEVATED). Pick one source of truth. (2) The two cold-start gates (calibrating vs sufficient_baseline) flatly contradict each other on 25 rows. (3) Detection is heavily quantized — dozens of instruments snap to identical values, and 9 'top movers' are 2-signal micro-floats whose abnormality is pure noise. (4) interpretation is a hardcoded identical string and the payload has double-encoded-UTF8 mojibake.

NET JUDGMENT: The tier system is directionally honest about a quiet market but is NOT tracked accurately or surfaced coherently — the headline tier is uninformative, the cold-start flags are inconsistent, thin-coverage noise pollutes the mover ranking, and per-instrument 'reads' (interpretation, floor det/conf) are partly templated rather than measured. Fix priority: reconcile calibrating<->sufficient_baseline, collapse the three tier fields to one, gate abnormality on a minimum signal count, and fix the UTF-8 re-encoding.

benchmarks.name: Wikipedia / Wikimedia Pageviews API (AQS REST v1) — RECOMMENDED PRIMARY ANCHOR
benchmarks.measures: Daily (and hourly/monthly) human pageviews to a specific Wikipedia article — i.e. how many real people actively looked up a named entity/topic. The most widely-cited academic proxy for 'public attention/concern' (used across sociology, epidemiology, politics, economics). For the no-gap test it is decisive: a genuinely mainstream topic almost always has a high-volume, stable Wikipedia article; a niche/pre-arrival topic has thin or no article traffic.
benchmarks.cost_licensing: FREE. Data is licensed CC0 1.0 (public domain) — the strongest possible licensing position, fully usable commercially with zero restriction. No API key required. No fixed rate limit; the only ask is to serialize requests (wait for one to finish before the next) so you don't threaten service stability. Endpoint: GET https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/metrics/pageviews/per-article/{wiki}/all-access/all-agents/{Article}/daily/{YYYYMMDD}/{YYYYMMDD}
benchmarks.non_circular: FULLY non-circular. Pageviews come from Wikimedia's own server traffic logs — an entirely independent population (people typing/clicking on Wikipedia) and an independent platform NowTrendIn does not collect from. It shares NO inputs with the Gradient Score (no RSS feeds, no X volume scan, no blog GHOST_FEEDS, no Detection/Confidence components). It cannot leak into our own signals, so it can validate direction without contaminating the score.
benchmarks.how_to_use: WIRE AS THE NO-GAP MAINSTREAM ANCHOR. (1) For each scored topic, resolve it to a canonical Wikipedia article (search/title-normalize; quarantine unresolved to a review queue like the ingestion gate does). (2) Pull trailing ~90d daily pageviews and compute a 'mainstream-attention level' = log10(median daily views) plus a stability metric (low coefficient of variation = established, not spiking). (3) Define the ground-truth label: a topic is ALREADY-MAINSTREAM if median daily pageviews exceed a high percentile of the corpus (e.g. >~p80 of all tracked articles) AND traffic is stable rather than freshly spiking. (4) The no-gap test passes when topics NowTrendIn classifies as mainstream/ESTABLISHED (in topic_lifecycle / the stage waterfall) line up with high+stable pageview level, and topics we call early/pre-arrival sit LOW on pageviews. (5) Store the pageview level in a separate calibration table (e.g. wiki_attention_anchor), NEVER as a Gradient-Score input — it is a held-out label only.

benchmarks.name: GDELT DOC 2.0 API (TimelineVol mode)
benchmarks.measures: Share of WORLDWIDE news coverage (broadcast + print + web, 100+ languages) matching a query, by day/hour/15-min — expressed as a PERCENT of all global coverage GDELT monitors, not raw counts. A direct measure of mainstream MEDIA attention (vs Wikipedia's audience attention). Excellent complement: Wikipedia = demand-side (people looking it up), GDELT = supply-side (media talking about it).
benchmarks.cost_licensing: FREE. DOC 2.0 API is open, no key, JSON/CSV/JSONP output, rolling 90-day window. Full historical archive (back to 1979) is free via Google BigQuery. Underlying GDELT data is openly licensed for analysis. No commercial paywall on the DOC API.
benchmarks.non_circular: Largely non-circular but needs care. GDELT monitors a far broader, global, multilingual media universe than NowTrendIn's curated trusted-direct RSS roster (El Pais, TechCrunch, BBC, CNBC, etc.). The risk: a handful of our RSS sources also appear in GDELT's corpus, so it is not 100% independent. Mitigate by treating GDELT's percent-of-global-coverage as the signal — at global scale our ~15 feeds are negligible, so overlap is immaterial. Keep it as a directional cross-check, secondary to Wikipedia.
benchmarks.how_to_use: SECONDARY MAINSTREAM CONFIRMATION. Use TimelineVol percent-coverage as a second independent axis on the no-gap test: a true mainstream topic should be HIGH on BOTH Wikipedia pageviews AND GDELT coverage-share. Disagreement is diagnostic — high GDELT + low Wikipedia = media-pushed but no public pull (often pre-arrival or astroturf); high Wikipedia + low GDELT = durable public interest without fresh news (established/evergreen). Log both in the calibration table; never feed into the score.

benchmarks.name: Google Trends API (alpha, July 2025) / relative search interest
benchmarks.measures: Relative search interest for a term on a consistent 0-scaled index across requests (5 years of history in the alpha). Search demand is a strong attention proxy and the exact benchmark NowTrendIn's accuracy ledger already measures lead-time against.
benchmarks.cost_licensing: Official API is FREE but ALPHA + gated — access opened on a rolling, very-limited-tester basis through 2025-2026; you must be approved. Terms reserve Google's right to set limits at sole discretion; pricing/rate-limits still TBD. Practical interim access is via paid scrapers (SerpApi/Apify/Scrapingdog) or the legacy Apify Google Trends path NowTrendIn already uses.
benchmarks.non_circular: PARTIALLY circular for NowTrendIn — IMPORTANT CAVEAT. The accuracy ledger already uses Google Trends as the breakout ground-truth (LED lead-time vs Trends). If you ALSO use Trends to calibrate the no-gap mainstream test you are anchoring to the same external series twice, which over-fits to Google's notion of attention. Keep Trends as the BREAKOUT/arrival-timing anchor (its established role) and use Wikipedia+GDELT for the MAINSTREAM-level anchor, so the two calibration jobs stay independent of each other.
benchmarks.how_to_use: KEEP IN ITS CURRENT LANE (breakout timing), do NOT reuse for no-gap. If you want a search-demand mainstream signal, derive it from Wikipedia/GDELT instead to avoid double-anchoring. If/when the official API graduates from alpha, swap the Apify scrape for it to de-risk ToS.

benchmarks.name: GoogleBooks / Ngram Viewer (arrival-lag anchor)
benchmarks.measures: Frequency of a term across Google's scanned-books corpus by year — culturomics. Captures when a term entered the durable, edited written record. Useful specifically for the ARRIVAL-LAG concept: the gap between when NowTrendIn detects a topic and when it shows up in books = how early we were relative to the slowest, most-mainstream medium.
benchmarks.cost_licensing: FREE. Dataset is CC BY 3.0 (attribution required, commercial OK). Bulk n-gram files downloadable; an unofficial JSON endpoint (books.google.com/ngrams/json) exists for ad-hoc pulls.
benchmarks.non_circular: Non-circular (books corpus shares nothing with our feeds) BUT temporally useless for live calibration: the corpus is annual and lags by years, with infrequent rebuilds. It validates the THESIS retrospectively ('we were N months/years ahead of the book record') but cannot anchor an ongoing weekly loop.
benchmarks.how_to_use: RETROSPECTIVE THESIS VALIDATION ONLY. For a basket of historical topics, measure detection-date vs first-meaningful-Ngram-year to demonstrate lead time for investor/marketing claims. Do not wire into the live calibration loop.

benchmarks.name: Reddit API (community attention)
benchmarks.measures: Post/comment volume and subreddit activity for a topic — grassroots/community attention, often an early-signal layer that precedes mainstream.
benchmarks.cost_licensing: PAID + opaque. Commercial access ~ $12,000/month for up to 50M calls, ~ $0.24 per 1,000 calls beyond; enterprise terms by negotiation, use-case review required. No free commercial tier at scale.
benchmarks.non_circular: Non-circular IF NowTrendIn does not already ingest Reddit (it is not in the source roster), but it measures EARLY/niche attention — the wrong construct for the no-gap MAINSTREAM test. Better suited to validating the early-detection (Dark Matter) side than the already-mainstream side.
benchmarks.how_to_use: OPTIONAL early-signal cross-check, not a mainstream anchor. Given cost + opacity and that it probes the niche end, deprioritize for the no-gap test. If used at all, treat as a Dark-Matter-pathway validator, held out from the score.

benchmarks.name: X / Twitter API (real-time social attention)
benchmarks.measures: Real-time post volume/engagement for a topic — social-media attention velocity.
benchmarks.cost_licensing: PAID + expensive. Pay-per-use default ($0.005/read, capped 2M reads/mo); Pro $5,000/mo; Enterprise $42,000-$50,000+/mo for full streams.
benchmarks.non_circular: CIRCULAR for NowTrendIn — DISQUALIFIED as an anchor. The engine already scans X every 6h (volume scan + author-gradient pulls, X_MONTHLY_POST_BUDGET). X data is an INPUT to the Gradient Score, so using X to validate the score is exactly the circular-metric failure the integrity standard forbids (a validator must use inputs independent of what it validates).
benchmarks.how_to_use: DO NOT USE as a calibration anchor — it would validate the score with the score's own inputs. Keep X strictly as a scoring input, never as ground truth.

benchmarks.name: Media-monitoring indices (MIT/Harvard Media Cloud; commercial vendors)
benchmarks.measures: Media Cloud: open-source index of millions of news stories, story-count attention over time per topic — similar construct to GDELT but research-grade and curated. Commercial alternatives (Meltwater, Brandwatch, NewsWhip) sell proprietary 'media attention/buzz' indices.
benchmarks.cost_licensing: Media Cloud = FREE / open (academic, API access). Commercial monitoring indices = PAID (enterprise contracts, often $10k-$100k+/yr) and frequently licensing-restricted for redistribution.
benchmarks.non_circular: Media Cloud is non-circular and independent; its weakness is overlap with the same news universe as GDELT (pick one to avoid redundancy). Commercial 'buzz' indices are often BLACK-BOX and may themselves blend social+search signals — that opacity risks hidden circularity and fails the licensed-transparent-source standard. Prefer open Media Cloud or GDELT over black-box vendors.
benchmarks.how_to_use: Use Media Cloud as a FREE swap-in/redundancy check for GDELT on the media-supply axis. Avoid black-box commercial indices as anchors (can't prove non-circularity; licensing blocks redistribution of the ground truth).

benchmarks.no_gap_anchor: BEST FREE, LICENSABLE ANCHOR FOR THE NO-GAP MAINSTREAM TEST = Wikipedia / Wikimedia Pageviews API (AQS REST v1). It is the textbook attention ground-truth in published research, it is genuinely free, and — critically — it is CC0 1.0 (public domain), the cleanest possible licensing for a commercial product to ingest and even resurface. It is fully non-circular: pageviews are Wikimedia's own server-traffic on a platform NowTrendIn does not collect from, sharing zero inputs with the Gradient Score (no RSS, no X scan, no blog feeds, no Detection/Confidence). For the no-gap test specifically, it is near-ideal: an already-mainstream topic reliably has a high-volume, STABLE Wikipedia article, while a niche or pre-arrival topic has thin or no article traffic — so 'did NowTrendIn correctly recognize this is already mainstream?' maps almost directly onto 'is trailing-90d median pageviews high and stable?'. Pair it with GDELT TimelineVol (free, percent-of-global-coverage) as the independent media-supply second axis: true mainstream should score HIGH on BOTH the demand axis (Wikipedia, audience) and the supply axis (GDELT, media). Reserve Google Trends for its EXISTING role as the breakout/arrival-timing anchor in the accuracy ledger — do NOT reuse it for no-gap, or you double-anchor to the same Google series. Disqualify X/Twitter outright: it is already a scoring INPUT, so using it as ground truth is the exact circular-metric failure the integrity standard forbids.
benchmarks.calibration_loop:
PROPOSED ONGOING, NON-CIRCULAR CALIBRATION LOOP (weekly, mirrors the existing accuracy-ledger cadence):

STEP 0 — Hard firewall (the rule that keeps it non-circular): the external anchors (Wikipedia pageviews, GDELT coverage-share) are written ONLY to a dedicated held-out table (e.g. attention_anchor) and are NEVER read by any Gradient-Score component, calibration weight, dual-pathway gate, or serve_payload. They label/validate direction; they are never folded in as an input the score then claims to predict. (Integrity standard: a validator must use inputs independent of what it validates.)

STEP 1 — Resolve: for each scored topic in the 90-day window, resolve a canonical Wikipedia article (title-normalize + search; unresolved topics go to a format_review_queue-style quarantine, never guessed — same condition-precedent discipline as the ingestion gate).

STEP 2 — Pull anchors (free): trailing ~90d daily pageviews per article (AQS REST), plus GDELT TimelineVol percent-coverage for the same span. Compute (a) mainstream-LEVEL = log10(median daily pageviews), (b) STABILITY = low coefficient of variation (established, not a fresh spike), (c) GDELT coverage-share level.

STEP 3 — Label: tag each topic ALREADY-MAINSTREAM if pageview level is above a high corpus percentile (e.g. >p80) AND stable AND GDELT-coverage high; tag EARLY/PRE-ARRIVAL if low+rising. These labels are derived purely from external data.

STEP 4 — Score the NO-GAP test: compare NowTrendIn's own mainstream/ESTABLISHED classification (topic_lifecycle + stage waterfall + saturation) against the external ALREADY-MAINSTREAM label. Compute a confusion matrix: the key failure is FALSE-EARLY (we called something an emerging signal when Wikipedia+GDELT say it's already mainstream — a no-gap miss). Report no-gap precision/recall, exactly like the accuracy ledger reports breakout hit-rate.

STEP 5 — Calibrate DIRECTION only: if false-early rate is rising, that is a signal to adjust the SATURATION / ESTABLISHED-discount thresholds inside the engine — but the adjustment is made by a human (or the calibration auditor) reading the report and tuning internal parameters; the external number is NOT regressed into the score. The anchor moves the threshold; it never becomes a feature.

STEP 6 — Drift alarm: like catchall_floor_log's IMPROVING/STABLE/WORSENING, store each weekly no-gap precision row and alert on WORSENING (false-early climbing > a set delta) — early warning that the engine is starting to mislabel mainstream topics as emerging.

WHY IT STAYS NON-CIRCULAR PERMANENTLY: (1) anchors live in a held-out table the score can't read; (2) the chosen anchors (Wikipedia, GDELT) share no inputs with the score, unlike X (an input) or Google Trends (already used for breakout); (3) the loop adjusts internal thresholds via human-reviewed reports, never auto-regresses the external series into the model; (4) the two calibration jobs are kept on different external series (Trends=breakout timing, Wikipedia/GDELT=mainstream level) so neither over-fits one provider's attention definition.
benchmarks.detail:
SOURCES (markdown links):
- Wikimedia Pageviews / AQS REST API: https://doc.wikimedia.org/generated-data-platform/aqs/analytics-api/reference/page-views.html ; getting started https://doc.wikimedia.org/generated-data-platform/aqs/analytics-api/documentation/getting-started.html ; access policy https://doc.wikimedia.org/generated-data-platform/aqs/analytics-api/documentation/access-policy.html (CC0 data, no fixed rate limit, serialize requests)
- Wikipedia pageviews as attention ground-truth (research): https://meta.wikimedia.org/wiki/Research:Quantifying_the_global_attention_to_public_health_threats_through_Wikipedia_pageview_data
- GDELT DOC 2.0 API (TimelineVol, free, JSON/CSV, 90d window): https://blog.gdeltproject.org/gdelt-doc-2-0-api-debuts/ ; project https://www.gdeltproject.org/ ; free-data context https://dataresearchtools.com/gdelt-project-for-news-data-2026-free-alternative-to-newsapi/
- Google Trends API alpha (July 2025, gated, relative interest): https://developers.google.com/search/blog/2025/07/trends-api ; docs https://developers.google.com/search/apis/trends
- Google Ngram (CC BY 3.0, annual corpus, JSON endpoint): https://books.google.com/ngrams/ ; API note https://jameshfisher.com/2018/11/25/google-ngram-api/
- Reddit API pricing (~$12k/mo, ~$0.24/1k calls, negotiated): https://sellbery.com/blog/how-much-does-the-reddit-api-cost-in-2025/ ; https://data365.co/blog/reddit-api-pricing
- X/Twitter API pricing (pay-per-use; Pro $5k/mo; Enterprise $42k-$50k+/mo): https://www.xpoz.ai/blog/guides/understanding-twitter-api-pricing-tiers-and-alternatives/ ; https://twitterapi.io/blog/twitter-api-pricing-2025

KEY DECISION SUMMARY:
1) PRIMARY no-gap anchor = Wikipedia Pageviews API (free, CC0, fully non-circular, the standard attention ground-truth; mainstream topics = high+stable pageviews).
2) SECONDARY axis = GDELT TimelineVol (free, percent-of-global-coverage, independent media-supply view). Mainstream = high on BOTH.
3) Google Trends stays as the BREAKOUT-timing anchor it already is in the accuracy ledger — do not reuse for no-gap (double-anchoring/over-fit to one provider).
4) X/Twitter DISQUALIFIED as anchor — it is already a scoring INPUT (6h scan), so using it as ground truth is the forbidden circular-metric pattern.
5) Reddit (paid, opaque) and Ngram (annual lag) are NOT live anchors: Reddit is the wrong construct (niche/early) + costly; Ngram is retrospective thesis-proof only.
6) Media-monitoring: prefer FREE open Media Cloud over black-box commercial 'buzz' indices (the latter can't prove non-circularity and block redistribution).

INTEGRITY GUARDRAIL (matches the project's no-circular-metrics + integrity standard): external anchors are written to a HELD-OUT calibration table (e.g. attention_anchor), never read by the score, never regressed into a weight or dual-pathway gate. They validate DIRECTION (precision/recall of the no-gap classification) and trip a WORSENING drift alarm; threshold tuning is human-reviewed, never auto-fit. This preserves the before-it-arrives thesis while finally giving the no-gap mainstream test an objective, free, licensable ground truth.

NO new code was written or files modified — this is research only.
